_meta.title: Lock-Free Data Structures — Treiber Stack, MS Queue
_meta.note: Two pre-lesson and two post-lesson questions.

questions.question: What is the defining guarantee of a lock-free data structure?
options: Every thread makes progress in a bounded number of steps.
At least one thread makes progress in any finite time interval.
No thread ever needs to wait for another thread.
The data structure uses no atomic operations at all.
questions.explanation: Lock-free guarantees system-wide progress: at least one thread makes forward progress in any finite time interval. Option A describes wait-free (a stronger guarantee). Option C is the goal but not the formal definition. Option D is impossible — lock-free structures rely on atomic CAS.

questions.question: What problem does the dummy node in a Michael-Scott queue solve?
options: It prevents memory allocation on every enqueue.
It ensures head and tail are never null, eliminating empty-queue special cases in CAS logic.
It doubles the throughput by allowing batch enqueues.
It allows the queue to grow beyond the available memory.
questions.explanation: The dummy node is an always-present sentinel that head always points to. This means head and tail are never null, which eliminates the need for special-case CAS when the queue is empty. Without it, an empty queue with null head/tail would require conditional logic around every CAS.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid strategy for handling the ABA problem in lock-free data structures?
options: Tagged pointers (packing an ABA counter with the pointer).
Hazard pointers (marking in-use pointers before dereferencing).
Using a mutex to protect all pointer loads.
Epoch-based reclamation (deferring free until all threads have left the epoch).
questions.explanation: Using a mutex defeats the entire purpose of lock-free programming — you reintroduce contention, convoying, and priority inversion. Tagged pointers, hazard pointers, and epoch-based reclamation are all valid ABA-mitigation strategies that preserve lock-freedom.

questions.question: In the Treiber stack's push operation, what happens when the CAS on the head pointer fails?
options: The thread blocks until the head pointer becomes available.
The thread retries: it reloads the head pointer, relinks the new node's next, and attempts CAS again.
The operation is forwarded to a dedicated service thread.
The data structure falls back to a mutex-based stack for this operation.
questions.explanation: CAS failure means another thread modified head between our load and our CAS. We simply loop: reload head, set node.next to the new head, and retry CAS. This is the standard lock-free retry pattern. The thread never blocks — it just tries again with fresh values.

questions.question: What does 'lock-free' mean in the context of data structures?
options: The data structure doesn't use any synchronization
At least one thread is guaranteed to make progress in a bounded number of steps, even if other threads are suspended
The data structure uses only atomic variables
The data structure is faster than all locked alternatives
questions.explanation: Lock-free guarantees system-wide progress: if any thread is suspended, the remaining threads can still complete their operations. This is stronger than obstruction-free but weaker than wait-free (which guarantees per-thread progress). Lock-free structures use CAS loops rather than locks.
